coredata.dc:title: An electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for the ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 based on gold nanorods/graphene quantum dots-modified poly(indole-6-carboxylic acid)/flower-gold nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Yan
Zhao, Xiaoqian
Tian, Ya
Guo, Qingfu
Li, Chunxiang
Nie, Guangming
coredata.dc:description: A simple electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor is designed for the ultrasensitive determination of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) based on poly(indole-6-carboxylic acid)/flower-gold (PICA/F-Au) nanocomposite, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanorods (AuNRs). The PICA/F-Au nanocomposite with a core-shell structure shows a satisfactory electrical conductivity. In addition, GQDs is employed as a superior luminescent material due to the high ECL activity. AuNRs, possessing the good conductivity, is utilized for immobilizing AFB1 aptamer chains to improve sensitivity. The easy-to-design sensing platform shows good ECL performance and response to AFB1. Under the optimal conditions, this ECL aptasensor exhibits a wide dynamic linear range from 0.01 to 100 ng mL− 1 with a low detection limit of 0.00375 ng mL− 1. Simultaneously, this sensor possesses satisfactory stability, selectivity and reproducibility, and it also has satisfactory accuracy and reliability in real sample analysis. All these advantages provide a promising prospective for food safety detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence aptasensor
Poly(indole-6-carboxylic acid)/flower-gold
Graphene quantum dots
Gold nanorods
Aflatoxin B1

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of PICA/F-Au nanocomposite, AuNRs, and GQDs
Characterization of PICA, F-Au and PICA/F-Au nanocomposite
Stability, reproducibility and specificity of ECL aptasensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
Qingdao University of Science and Technology
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of PICA (A), F-Au (B), PICA/F-Au nanocomposite (C) and TEM images of PICA/F-Au nanocomposite (D), GQDs (E), AuNRs (F).

(A) EIS of the modified electrodes: (a) PICA/F-Au/GCE, (b) GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE, (c) AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE, (d) Aptamer/AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE, (e) BSA/Aptamer/AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE, (f) AFB1/BSA/Aptamer/AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE; (B) ECL responses of (a) Bare GCE, (b) PICA/F-Au/GCE, (c) AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE, (d) Aptamer/AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE, (e) BSA/Aptamer/AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE, (f) AFB1/Aptamer/AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE; (C) ECL response signals of different AFB1 concentrations (0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 2.0, 10.0, 25.0, 50.0, 100 ng mL− 1); (D) the linear relationship between ECL intensity and the logarithm of AFB1 concentration.
(A) Three-cycle stability test of ECL response at different concentrations, (B) ECL response signals of 15 consecutive cycles, (C) reproducibility and (D) specificity of ECL aptasensor.
The construction process of ECL aptasensor based on PICA/F-Au nanocomposite.
Comparisons of the sensor performance based on different methods.
The recovery experiments of this ECL aptasensor and HPLC-FL in real samples.

head.ce:title: An electrochemiluminescence aptasensor for the ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 based on gold nanorods/graphene quantum dots-modified poly(indole-6-carboxylic acid)/flower-gold nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, MOE; State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering; College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
MOE; State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering; College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optic-Electric Sensing and Analytical chemistry for Life Science, MOE, State Key Laboratory Base of Eco-chemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao, 266042, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple electrochemiluminescence (ECL) aptasensor is designed for the ultrasensitive determination of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) based on poly(indole-6-carboxylic acid)/flower-gold (PICA/F-Au) nanocomposite, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and gold nanorods (AuNRs). The PICA/F-Au nanocomposite with a core-shell structure shows a satisfactory electrical conductivity. In addition, GQDs is employed as a superior luminescent material due to the high ECL activity. AuNRs, possessing the good conductivity, is utilized for immobilizing AFB1 aptamer chains to improve sensitivity. The easy-to-design sensing platform shows good ECL performance and response to AFB1. Under the optimal conditions, this ECL aptasensor exhibits a wide dynamic linear range from 0.01 to 100 ng mL− 1 with a low detection limit of 0.00375 ng mL− 1. Simultaneously, this sensor possesses satisfactory stability, selectivity and reproducibility, and it also has satisfactory accuracy and reliability in real sample analysis. All these advantages provide a promising prospective for food safety detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), a class Ι carcinogen defined by the International Cancer Research Agency (IARC) [1], is present in agricultural and animal products. AFB1 is considered as one harmful substance because of its carcinogenicity, teratogenicity and embryo toxicity [2,3]. Due to the characteristics of colorlessness and thermal stability, it is difficult to determine if food has been contaminated by AFB1 [4]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop fast and sensitive analytical methods that can effectively detect AFB1 levels.

Currently, many analytical techniques for AFB1 detection have been proposed [5-7]. For instance, Chauhan et al. developed an electrochemical quartz crystal microbalance to quantitatively detect AFB1 in peanuts [8]. Krittayavathananon et al. reported a high sensitivity impedance biosensor for AFB1 detection [9]. Lin et al. achieved the detection of AFB1 by constructing a photoelectrochemical immunosensor [10]. Although these methods are inexpensive and easy to operate, their sensitivity needs to be further improved. Up to now, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) method, with multiple advantages of fast sample analysis, strong specificity, high sensitivity and low background noise [11], has attracted considerable attentions in the field of medical diagnosis and food detection [12].

Luminescent material is an indispensable part of ECL measurement. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), owing to the multiple advantages of low toxicity, stable ECL signal, high electrical conductivity, and good biocompatibility [13-15], has been considered as a hot topic in the field of ECL. Furthermore, metal nanomaterials such as gold nanorods (AuNRs) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) can also promote the electron-transfer reaction. Particularly, due to the unique morphology, AuNRs show greater load capacity and better electrical conductivity [16]. Poly(indole-6-carboxylic acid) (PICA) is a matrix material during the construction of ECL sensors, which possess good stability and high electrochemical activity [17]. Although it owns various superiority such as good photoelectric property, high redox activity, and strong stability [18], its conductivity needs to be further improved. Previously, Yang et al. synthesized a poly(indole-6-carboxylic acid)/flower-like Au nanoparticles (PICA/FGNs) composite for the construction of “signal-on” electrochemiluminescence immunosensor to detect prostate-specific antigen [19]. The introduction of flower-like Au nanoparticles (FGNs) can adjust the physical and chemical properties of composite, resulting in a great increase in electrical conductivity [20]. Simultaneously, this composite has a large specific surface area, which can increase the loading capacity of bioactive substance [21]. Regrettably, the previous work has the disadvantages of tedious probe preparation and complicated construction process, which limits the development of ECL sensor in terms of simplicity and convenience [19].

In order to solve the problems in previous work, here, a simple ECL aptasensor was established for the quantitative evaluation of AFB1. In this study, GQDs and AuNRs were directly modified on the surface of PICA/F-Au nanocomposite, avoiding the complicated probe preparation processes. The combination of GQDs and AuNRs produced a dual signal amplification effect, making the ECL aptasensor present a higher sensitivity for food analysis and detection.
The materials and apparatus used in this experiment are shown in Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PICA/F-Au nanocomposite, AuNRs, and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: PICA/F-Au nanocomposite, GQDs and AuNRs were prepared referring to the previous literatures [22-24]. The specific details were provided in Supplementary Information.

Construction procedure of this simple ECL aptasensor is shown in Scheme 1. First of all, F-Au was modified on the GCE by potentiostatic method with a potential of 0.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl and a deposition time of 60 min. After that, PICA/F-Au/GCE was obtained through electrochemical polymerization process, in which the potential was set to 1.37 V vs. Ag/AgCl and the quantity of electric charge was 10 mC. Then, 10 μL GQDs solution and 10 μL AuNRs solution were added respectively to form AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE. In this process, positively-charged GQDs and positively-charged AuNRs were firmly combined, thereby fixing AuNRs to the electrodes. Subsequently, 0.1 ng mL− 1 AFB1 aptamer chain (10 μL) was introduced onto the electrode surface and incubated for 12 h at 4 °C. After washing the electrode with PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4), the unbound aptamer chains were removed and obtained Aptamer/AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE. Furthermore, in order to block the non-specific binding sites, the above electrode was immersed in bovine serum albumin (BSA) (2 wt%) solution for 1 h at 37 °C and generated BSA/Aptamer/AuNRs/GQDs/PICA/F-Au/GCE. Finally, the modified electrode was incubated with different concentrations of AFB1 for 80 min at 37 °C, and then corresponding ECL intensity was obtained (Scheme 1). The sequence of AFB1 aptamer chain and the incubation time of AFB1 aptamer were provided in Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PICA, F-Au and PICA/F-Au nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The structure and morphology of PICA, F-Au and PICA/F-Au nanocomposite were investigated by SEM and TEM. It can be observed visually that PICA film has a porous network structure with different granular protrusions (Fig. 1 A) and F-Au possesses a unique flower-like structure which is advantageous for increasing its loading area (Fig. 1B). Furthermore, PICA/F-Au nanocomposite has the structural characteristics of both PICA and F-Au, which presents a uniform and complete nanoflower structure (Fig. 1C). This structure can increase the electron transfer rate and provide a larger surface area for the immobilization of AFB1. According to TEM image (Fig. 1D), PICA/F-Au nanocomposite shows a unique core-shell structure, which is consistent with its SEM image. This result also proves that the PICA/F-Au nanocomposite is successfully synthesized.

TEM image of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1E. GQDs are monodispersed in the solution and their average diameter is about 4 nm on average. This structure may facilitate the movement of electrons between electrodes and GQDs. And in UV-vis absorption spectra (Fig. S1A), GQDs have a distinct absorption peak at about 360 nm (curve a). Citric acid has no obvious absorption peak (curve b). This difference is attributed to the rich sp2 structure and oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs [25]. As mentioned above, GQDs are successfully prepared [23]. In addition, TEM image and UV-vis absorption spectrum are also used to analyze the structural characteristics of AuNRs. According to TEM (Fig. 1F), AuNRs have rod-like structures with a particle size of 20 nm. According to UV-vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S1B), AuNRs have two absorption peaks at 540 and 715 nm, which demonstrates that AuNRs are well prepared [22].

The construction process of ECL aptasensor is studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in the presence of 5.0 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.1 M KCl. Fig. 2 A shows the EIS results at different electrode modification steps and the equivalent circuit is provided in the inset. There are four factors in the equivalent circuit: the Warburg (Zw), the electron transfer resistance (Ret), the resistance of solution (Rs), and the double layer capacitance (Cd). Especially, the Ret corresponds to the semicircle diameter of each curve, which varies with the change of modified electrode interface. As depicted in Fig. 2A, compared with PICA/F-Au modified electrode (curve a), the Ret value decreases slightly when GQDs are assembled on the surface (curve b). This result is attributed to the small lateral dimension of GQDs which is below 10 nm (about 4 nm, Fig. 1E). The quantum confinement and boundary effects make it conductive [26]. Furthermore, when AuNRs are introduced again, the Ret value decreases significantly (curve c) because AuNRs can accelerate the electrons transfer, thereby improving the ECL intensity [25]. The above phenomenon indicates that GQDs and AuNRs are both decorated on modified electrodes. After AFB1 aptamer chain is connected (curve d), the Ret values of electrode increase. Moreover, the Ret values are also significantly increased after residual active sites are blocked by BSA (curve e). This is mainly because the presence of protein can hinder the exchange of electrons which are between electrode surface and electrochemical probe. Thus, AFB1 aptamer and BSA are successfully connected. Finally, when modified electrode is incubated with AFB1, the Ret values are further increased (curve f). The reason for this phenomenon is that the appearance of AFB1 makes the steric resistance to be greater, which slows down the electron transfer rate and promotes the increase of impedance. The above results demonstrate that ECL aptasensor is constructed successfully, which is in accordance with Scheme 1.

Furthermore, Fig. 2B shows the ECL responses of different modified electrodes after each step. When the bare GCE (curve a) is modified with PICA/F-Au, a slight ECL signal appears (curve b). Subsequently, with the addition of GQDs and AuNRs, ECL signal increases obviously (curve c) because the combination of GQDs and AuNRs produces a dual signal amplification effect, greatly enhancing the conductivity of the electrode. After the incubation of aptamer (curve d), ECL signal is reduced instead. The above phenomenon is attributed to the steric resistance of aptamer chains. When residual active sites are blocked by BSA (curve e), the steric resistance is further increased, causing the ECL signal to further decrease. Finally, with the adjunction of AFB1 (curve f), ECL signal decreases obviously due to the large steric resistance of AFB1. All above results are consistent with the EIS characterizations of ECL aptasensor, further indicating the successful fabrication of this ECL sensor.

In order to obtain an ideal performance of the ECL immunosensor, the incubation time between AFB1 and aptamer, the aptamer concentration, the buffer pH, and the incubation time of aptamer were investigated. The detailed descriptions were provided in Supplementary Information. According to Fig. S2, 80 min was selected as the optimal incubation time between AFB1 and aptamer, 0.5 μM was selected as the optimal aptamer concentration, 7.4 was selected as the optimal buffer pH, and 12 h was selected as the optimal incubation time of aptamer, respectively.

Under the above optimal conditions, the ECL behavior of fabricated aptasensor was recorded with different concentrations of AFB1. In the following study, the ECL aptasensor was fabricated on the Au-S bond between AuNRs and AFB1 aptamer chains. According to Fig. 2C, the steric hindrance effect weakened the ability of electron transfer and caused a decreased ECL response with the increase of AFB1 concentration. Correspondingly, the ECL intensity was negatively correlated with the logarithm of AFB1 concentration from 0.01 ng mL− 1 to100 ng mL− 1 (Fig. 2D). The linear correlation equation is Y = 8319.94–2804.28 lg c, and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.00375 ng mL− 1 which is calculated by the 3σ method. The above data confirms that the sensing platform in this work, as a simple and fast ECL method, exhibits excellent sensitivity for AFB1 detection.

Table 1 illustrates the performance parameters of different sensors for AFB1 detection from literatures. Obviously, this fabricated ECL aptasensor exhibits a lower detection limit and a wider linear range, which provides a very promising approach for ultrasensitive detection of other important proteins in food. The main reasons for this phenomenon are as follows: (1) With good conductivity, GQDs provide high ECL intensity; (2) AuNRs can further accelerate the transition of electrons and enhance the ECL response of electrodes; (3) PICA/F-Au nanocomposite has dual advantages of F-Au and PICA, which provides a larger specific surface area and promotes the fixation of bioactive molecules. These factors lead to an improved detection effect of ECL aptasensor effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and specificity of ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The stability of ECL aptasensor is important for its practical application. Fig. 3
A shows the stability detection of different AFB1 concentrations. Each concentration is measured three times for comparisons. Obviously, the detected ECL response signals decrease steadily with the increase of AFB1 concentration. Fig. 3B provides the relationship between ECL signal and time, in which the detected ECL response signals are stable with the change of scanning time. These results indicate that ECL sensor has a good cycling stability. To evaluate the reproducibility of aptasensor, five electrodes are prepared in the same conditions to detect AFB1 (0.1 ng mL− 1) (Fig. 3C). The calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) is 2.97%, indicating a good reproducibility of ECL aptasensor. As shown in Fig. 3D, the interference study is performed using AFB1, Aflatoxin B2 (AFB2), Ochratoxin A (OTA), Aflatoxin M1 (AFM1) and their mixtures respectively. It can be found that the ECL response signal is lower with the presence of AFB1. The interfering substances have no obvious influences on ECL intensity. The above results illustrate that this constructed sensor has good selectivity for AFB1.

The actual application was evidenced by detecting AFB1 in peanut, maize and wheat. Initially, the moldy food samples were obtained by placing the clean food in a humid room temperature for one week. Subsequently, the clean and moldy samples were separated according to the previous work [34]. Typically, adding 2 g of above food samples to 2 mL methanol, shaking for 30 min, and centrifuging at 3000 rpm for 5 min. Repeat the above extraction process three times, collect all extracts, transfer them to a 5 mL PE tube, and dilute to the mark with methanol. Finally, 100 μL of the above sample solution was accurately taken for ECL analysis.

In this experiment, actual food samples were analyzed and tested using the standard addition method. 1 nM, 2 nM and 5 nM AFB1 standard solutions were added to the samples for actual detection. The presence of AFB1 was not found in the cleaned samples by detecting three food samples (peanut, maize and wheat). However, as shown in Table 2 , trace amounts of AFB1 were present in the moldy food samples. After the AFB1 standard solution was added, the recovery values were 97.1-111.8%, indicating that the ECL sensor can be used for real food analysis. In addition, the national standard method of high-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence (HPLC-FL) was used to evaluate the accuracy and reliability of this fabricated ECL aptasensor. As shown in Table 2, the recovery of HPLC-FL was 90.7-114.7%. Thus, these two methods showed the satisfactory recoveries, which meant the comparable accuracy of this aptasensor for real sample analysis. Based on these results, it can be concluded that this fabricated ECL aptasensor had satisfactory accuracy and reliability in real sample analysis.

Highly enhanced ECL intensity from GQDs/AuNRs-modified PICA/F-Au nanocomposite has been reported firstly. Compared with a pure PICA/F-Au nanocomposite, the GQDs/AuNRs-modified PICA/F-Au nanocomposite has a high and stable ECL response and a large specific surface area. Based on these properties, a simple ECL aptasensing platform is fabricated for AFB1 detection. With multiple signal amplification, the measured limit of detection is 0.00375 ng mL− 1 in the linear dynamic range of 0.01-100 ng mL− 1. In addition, such an aptasensor has some advantages including facile operation, high sensitivity, good selectivity and long-term stability. This proposed approach may provide an interesting ECL detection method for other biological samples.

Yan Lu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xiaoqian Zhao: Formal analysis, Data curation. Ya Tian: Investigation, Validation. Qingfu Guo: Methodology, Formal analysis. Chunxiang Li: Project administration, Resources, Methodology, Supervision. Guangming Nie: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51973102), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2019MB067) and Talent Fund of QUST (2019). Authors have declared that no conflict of interest exists.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104959.
